notes: Direction question: where are you going? Ka' = where; wáhse' = you go. The direction verb system in Kanien'kéha is rich — 'go', 'come from', and 'went to' are grammatically distinct.
Direction question: where did you come from? Distinguishes origin (where you came from) from destination (where you're going).
Direction question about a past action: where did you go? The -skwe' suffix marks past tense in this verb form.
Common greeting — 'hello', 'hi', or more literally 'still/yet' (an expression of ongoing life and presence). Used in both casual and formal contexts.
Deep thanks — 'niawenhkó:wa' carries more weight than a simple 'thank you'. It connects to the spirit of the Thanksgiving Address (Ohén:ton Karihwatéhkwen).

english: that's it / That's right / Well done
notes: Affirming expression — 'that's it', 'that's right', or 'well done'. Used in conversation and teaching to confirm understanding.

english: wonderful / Excellent (lit. it pleases the mind)
notes: Literally: 'it makes my mind happy/satisfied'. Used to express genuine delight or approval. Demonstrates how Kanien'kéha describes internal states through embodied metaphor.
Mild negation or correction — 'not quite', 'no', or 'that's not right'. Softer than a flat 'no'.
The primary negation particle. Iáh + verb = negated verb. Example: Iáh ká:tiens = I am not sitting. Negation is formed through a particle + verb structure rather than internal verb change.

english: it makes our minds happy / We give thanks
notes: The closing refrain of each section of the Thanksgiving Address — 'and so our minds are made happy' or 'and so we give thanks'. The same root as Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne ('it makes my mind happy') but in the first person plural inclusive.
From the Thanksgiving Address — acknowledging the earth. Ohwén:tsia = earth/ground; Ionkwarihó:ten = it takes care of us / it is our responsibility.
The standard greeting-and-inquiry pair. Shé:kon (hello) + tó:kens (how are you? / are you well?). The response is: Io'tenhró:ton (I am well) or Iah tó:kens (Not so well).

cite.text: Kontinónhstats dialogue curriculum · Mary McDonald, Kanehsatà:ke

english: i am well / I am good
notes: Response to 'how are you?' — I am well. Literally 'it is going along well with me'. Used in both casual and formal contexts.

cite.text: Kontinónhstats dialogue curriculum · Mary McDonald, Kanehsatà:ke

notes: Mild negation of wellbeing — 'not really', 'not so well'. You already know Iáh (negation particle) from Unit 3. Here tó:kens = well/OK, so Iáh tó:kens = not OK.

cite.text: Kontinónhstats dialogue curriculum · Mary McDonald, Kanehsatà:ke

english: thank you for what you did
notes: Extended thanks for a specific action. You know Niawenhkó:wa (thank you very much). na'teionkwarihón:ni = 'for what you/we did'. Useful in ceremonies and community contexts.

cite.text: Kontinónhstats dialogue curriculum · Mary McDonald, Kanehsatà:ke

notes: What is your name? — literally 'what is it they call you?'. One of the first questions in any greeting sequence.

cite.text: Kontinónhstats dialogue curriculum · Mary McDonald, Kanehsatà:ke

english: my name is [Name] (lit. they call me [Name])
notes: Response to 'what is your name?' — 'they call me [Name]'. Replace [Tsiionkwe] with your Kanien'kéha name or the name you wish to give.

cite.text: Kontinónhstats dialogue curriculum · Mary McDonald, Kanehsatà:ke

english: that is all / I am finished (closing)
notes: 'That is all / I am finished' — the closing phrase used at the end of a speech or a portion of the Thanksgiving Address. Onen' = now; ki' = just/only; wáhi = an affirmative particle. Together: 'now, that's it, truly'.

cite.text: Thanksgiving Address closing phrase · Kanehsatà:ke tradition

notes: What time is it? — a key practical phrase for daily life. Tsi = where/when; niiohtonhátie = it is at what point (in the day). The numbers you learned feed directly into time-telling.

cite.text: Kontinónhstats dialogue curriculum · Mary McDonald, Kanehsatà:ke

notes: The core 'what do you see?' question used throughout the Kontinónhstats curriculum. katkáhthos = I see (habitual). Nahò:ten = what. Used in lessons on shapes, animals, cultural figures, and objects.
groupLabel: what do you see — Nahò:ten katkáhthos?

notes: 'I see' — habitual form. Structure: k- (I) + atkáhthos (A-stem perception verb). Used as the answer verb in the 'what do you see?' dialogue pattern throughout the curriculum.
groupLabel: what do you see — Nahò:ten katkáhthos?

notes: Atatá:ho = Tadodaho / Tahtataho — the head chief of the Six Nations Confederacy in the founding era. A deeply tangled man (literally: his hair was said to be made of snakes) who was persuaded by the Peacemaker and Hiawatha to join the Confederacy. His title is still held today.
groupLabel: what do you see — Nahò:ten katkáhthos?

notes: Rononhshioní:ton = the Peacemaker (one form of the title). The same figure referred to as Tekanawí:ta. Seeing the Peacemaker in a lesson connects language to the founding narrative of the Confederacy.
groupLabel: what do you see — Nahò:ten katkáhthos?

notes: Ohnikakó:ten = what food / what kind. sé:ka's = you like (habitual). A key conversational question linking food vocabulary to preference expression.
groupLabel: what do you like? — Ohnikakó:ten sé:ka's?

notes: I like / I enjoy — habitual form. Structure: wak- (I, objective) + é:ka's (E-stem liking verb). Used with a noun: 'Onón:ta waké:ka's' = I like corn.
groupLabel: what do you like? — Ohnikakó:ten sé:ka's?

notes: Negated preference: Iáh (negation particle) + waké:ka' (I like). The negation particle Iáh you learned in Unit 3 applies to any verb — including preference verbs.
groupLabel: what do you like? — Ohnikakó:ten sé:ka's?

notes: Ohnikakó:ten = what kind / what type / what food. A flexible question word that combines with verbs and nouns to ask about category or preference.
groupLabel: what do you like? — Ohnikakó:ten sé:ka's?

english: how old are you? (lit. how many years are you?)
notes: Age question — literally 'how many winters (years) are you?' Niiawen'okón:'a = how many; niió:re' = years/winters. The word for year literally means 'winters' — the counting of years by the hardest season.

Year — literally 'winter(s)'. Kanien'kéha counts years by winters, not by solar cycles. This reflects the Haudenosaunee seasonal calendar where winter (Kenién:ke) is the structuring point of the year.
Age statement: [number] + niió:re' (years/winters) + wakén:iate (I am / I exist at). Example: Wísk óia'k niió:re' wakén:iate = I am fifty years old. wakén:iate is the first-person existence verb.
One o'clock — Énska (one) + niiohtonhátie (it is at that point in the day). The time system uses the numbers you already know combined with the time-of-day verb stem.
Formal 'what time is it?' — Nahò:ten (what) + niiohtonhátie (it is at that point). You already know tsi niiohtonhátie? (the short form). This fuller version is more common in careful speech.
Morning / daytime. Combining time-of-day expressions with activity verbs: 'ohnhiarí:ha ká:tiens' = I sit in the morning (I am usually sitting in the morning).
Tonight / this evening. Time-of-day nouns like ohsì:ka combine freely with any habitual verb to give the time at which the action occurs.
Today — literally 'our mind is present / it is now'. The word for 'today' in Kanien'kéha encodes presence and mindfulness rather than just a point on a calendar.
Not good / bad — Iáh (negation) + ohsì:io (good). Negation with the qualifier Iáh produces the opposite meaning. Same particle, same rule as negating verbs.
A long time — literally 'it is a big time'. The qualifier owá:nen (big/large) applied to time. Demonstrates how qualifiers combine with nouns and verbs to create new meanings.
Never — Iáh (negation) + ka'niká:ien (ever / any time). Stronger negation than a simple Iáh. 'Iáh ka'niká:ien katkáhthos' = I never see it.

english: i am going to my mother's house
notes: I am going to my mother's house. Full sentence: Ake'nistén:ha (my mother) + tsi (where/to) + tiakonónhsote' (her house stands) + wà:ke' (I am going). All components now known.

english: i am going to my father's house
notes: I am going to my father's house. Rake'ní:ha (my father) + tsi + thonónhsote' (his house stands) + wà:ke'.

english: i am going to my grandmother's house
notes: I am going to my grandmother's house. Aksóhtha (my grandmother) — you know this from the kinship lesson.

english: i am going to my grandfather's house
notes: I am going to my grandfather's house. Raksóhtha (my grandfather).

english: where are you going? / I am going (there)
notes: The core direction verb pair from Lesson 18. Ka' wáhse'? = where are you going? Wà:ke' = I am going (there). You know both from the direction verb lesson — here they appear as the full question-answer pair.

english: where did you come from? / I came from (there)
notes: The second direction verb pair. Ka' nontáhse'? = where did you come from? Non nontà:ke = I came from (there).

english: where did you go? / I went (there)
notes: The third direction verb pair. Ka' iéhse'skwe'? = where did you go? Iè:ke'skwe' = I went (there). The -skwe' suffix marks the incompletive past you know from the negation lessons.
Shé:kon — you know this as a greeting. Mary McDonald notes in Lesson 11 that shé:kon has multiple meanings: again, more, or hello. As a particle it expresses a return or addition.

cite.text: Kanehsatà:ke community usage; Maracle (1993) One Thousand Useful Mohawk Words

english: the one who is small / the little one
notes: The little one — tsi ionnhéhton. A descriptive phrase used as an endearment or to identify small things. Can be used with any noun: 'the little house', 'the little child'.

cite.text: Kanehsatà:ke community usage; Maracle (1993) One Thousand Useful Mohawk Words

english: you are good / you are well (colloquial)
notes: You are well / you are good — sathahkwí:ios. A more colloquial version of the wellness expression used in greetings.

cite.text: Kanehsatà:ke community usage; Maracle (1993) One Thousand Useful Mohawk Words

english: will you drink...? / Would you like to drink...?
notes: Will you drink...? — Khnekíra ken...? From Deering & Harries-Delisle: 'Enhsnekíra ken káwhe?' (will you drink a coffee?). A polite offering formula.

cite.text: Deering & Harries-Delisle, Mohawk: A Teaching Grammar (via kanienkeha.org)

notes: Tomorrow night — eniorhén'ne enió:karahwe'. A combination of tomorrow (eniorhén'ne) and tonight (enió:karahwe').

cite.text: Deering & Harries-Delisle, Mohawk: A Teaching Grammar (via kanienkeha.org)

notes: It is there — E'tho ká:yen. The response when the thing is away from the speaker. Compare tho (there) you know as a standalone particle.

cite.text: eCampusOntario OER — Mohawk Language Learning Resources (CC BY 4.0) · Six Nations Polytechnic

notes: I understand — Wake'nikonhrayén:ta's. The mind/heart (-nikonhr-) root + understanding suffix. Literally: my mind/heart grasps it. A crucial phrase for any language learner.

cite.text: eCampusOntario OER — Mohawk Language Learning Resources (CC BY 4.0) · Six Nations Polytechnic

notes: I don't understand — Yah tewake'nikonhrayén:ta's. Yah = no/not + te- (dualic negation) + the understanding verb. One of the most important phrases for a language learner.

cite.text: eCampusOntario OER — Mohawk Language Learning Resources (CC BY 4.0) · Six Nations Polytechnic

notes: I know — Wakateryén:tare. A general knowledge confirmation phrase. Slightly different from Wakaterién:tare (states of being) — this is the conversational form.

cite.text: eCampusOntario OER — Mohawk Language Learning Resources (CC BY 4.0) · Six Nations Polytechnic

notes: I don't know — Yah tewakateryén:tare. The negation of I know. One of the most commonly needed phrases in any language.

cite.text: eCampusOntario OER — Mohawk Language Learning Resources (CC BY 4.0) · Six Nations Polytechnic

notes: How do you say ___? — Oh ni:ioht tsi enshi:ron ___? A metalinguistic question indispensable for language learners. ni:ioht = how is it; tsi = that; enshi:ron = you will say.

cite.text: eCampusOntario OER — Mohawk Language Learning Resources (CC BY 4.0) · Six Nations Polytechnic

notes: What does ___ mean? — Oh naho:ten ken:ton ne ___? Another essential metalinguistic question for language learning.

cite.text: eCampusOntario OER — Mohawk Language Learning Resources (CC BY 4.0) · Six Nations Polytechnic

notes: Say it again — Seshi:ron. An important learning phrase when you haven't heard something clearly. se- (you, command) + shi:ron (say it).

cite.text: eCampusOntario OER — Mohawk Language Learning Resources (CC BY 4.0) · Six Nations Polytechnic

notes: I want — í:kehre. You know sahshá:wi (you want, command) from the true E-stem lesson. This is the first-person stative 'I want' form used in conversation.

cite.text: eCampusOntario OER — Mohawk Language Learning Resources (CC BY 4.0) · Six Nations Polytechnic

notes: I don't want — yah té:kehre. Negation of í:kehre (I want). Yah (no/not) + te- (dualic negation prefix) + é:kehre.

cite.text: eCampusOntario OER — Mohawk Language Learning Resources (CC BY 4.0) · Six Nations Polytechnic

notes: Are you hungry? — Satonhkaria'ks ken? sa- (you) + tonhkaria'ks (hungry root) + ken? (question particle). One of the most practical daily questions.

cite.text: eCampusOntario OER — Mohawk Language Learning Resources (CC BY 4.0) · Six Nations Polytechnic

notes: I'm not hungry yet — I:iah (no/not) + sotsi (so much) + arok (yet) + tekatonhkaria'ks (not hungry). A useful mealtime refusal phrase.

cite.text: eCampusOntario OER — Mohawk Language Learning Resources (CC BY 4.0) · Six Nations Polytechnic

notes: I have already eaten — O:nen (already/now) + ni:'i (I) + wakekhwenta:on (I ate/have eaten). The perfect aspect 'have eaten' form.

cite.text: eCampusOntario OER — Mohawk Language Learning Resources (CC BY 4.0) · Six Nations Polytechnic

notes: What will you eat? — Naho:ten (what) + ni:se' (you) + enseke (you will eat, future form). The en- prefix marks future tense — the first time the future prefix appears in conversation.

cite.text: eCampusOntario OER — Mohawk Language Learning Resources (CC BY 4.0) · Six Nations Polytechnic

english: what do you want to drink?
notes: What do you want to drink? — Naho:ten (what) + ensneki:ra (you will drink, future form). The drinking question at a meal.

cite.text: eCampusOntario OER — Mohawk Language Learning Resources (CC BY 4.0) · Six Nations Polytechnic

accepts: what do you want to drink?

english: do you like the taste of [food]?
notes: Do you like the taste of ___? — Se:kas ken ne ___. A food appreciation question. The tasting/liking-the-taste-of root -e:kas-.

cite.text: eCampusOntario OER — Mohawk Language Learning Resources (CC BY 4.0) · Six Nations Polytechnic

accepts: do you like the taste of

english: yes, I like the taste of [food]
notes: Yes, I like the taste of ___ — Hen (yes) + wakekas (I like the taste) + ne (the/that). A positive food response.

cite.text: eCampusOntario OER — Mohawk Language Learning Resources (CC BY 4.0) · Six Nations Polytechnic

english: no, I don't like the taste of [food]
notes: No, I don't like the taste of ___ — I:iah (no/not) + te- (dualic negation) + wakekas (I like the taste). A polite food refusal.

cite.text: eCampusOntario OER — Mohawk Language Learning Resources (CC BY 4.0) · Six Nations Polytechnic

english: is it delicious? / Is it good?
notes: Is it delicious? — Iawekon ken? iawekon (it is delicious) + ken? (question particle). From the food description vocabulary.

cite.text: eCampusOntario OER — Mohawk Language Learning Resources (CC BY 4.0) · Six Nations Polytechnic

notes: Hello, is everything peaceful? — Shé:kon (again/hello) + skennenkó: (great peace) + ken? (question). The full formal greeting from the eCampusOntario OER, showing that 'how are you?' in Kanien'kéha is literally asking about the state of peace.

cite.text: eCampusOntario OER — Mohawk Language Learning Resources (CC BY 4.0) · Six Nations Polytechnic

english: great peace for me / I am well (full form)
notes: Great peace for me — Skennen'kó:wa (great peace) + ní:'i (for me/I). The full formal wellness response. You know skén:nen (peace/peacefully) — Skennen'kó:wa is the augmented 'great peace' form.

cite.text: eCampusOntario OER — Mohawk Language Learning Resources (CC BY 4.0) · Six Nations Polytechnic

notes: And you? — Nok (and/then) + ní:se? (you). The follow-up question after someone says how they are. You know ní:se (you) from the pronouns lesson.

cite.text: eCampusOntario OER — Mohawk Language Learning Resources (CC BY 4.0) · Six Nations Polytechnic

notes: It is going well — Yoyanerátye. A common wellness response. Io'tenhró:ton (I am well) from Kontinónhstats is the version you know; this eCampusOntario form means roughly 'things are going well / it is proceeding nicely'.

cite.text: eCampusOntario OER — Mohawk Language Learning Resources (CC BY 4.0) · Six Nations Polytechnic

english: what is your name? (Six Nations form)
notes: What is your name? — Oh nahò:ten yesá:ya'ts? (Six Nations OER form). You know Kanié:ri iken'? from the Kontinónhstats/Kanehsatà:ke form. This eCampusOntario variant means 'what do they call you?' with a different verb root.

cite.text: eCampusOntario OER — Mohawk Language Learning Resources (CC BY 4.0) · Six Nations Polytechnic

notes: ___ is my name — ___ yónkya'ts. Six Nations OER name introduction. Compare [Name] akwé:kon (Kanehsatà:ke form). The root -ya'ts relates to naming/calling.

cite.text: eCampusOntario OER — Mohawk Language Learning Resources (CC BY 4.0) · Six Nations Polytechnic

notes: ___ is my last name — ___ wakhsenná:sere. A surname introduction. Most Kanien'kehá:ka families have both a traditional name and a family surname.

cite.text: eCampusOntario OER — Mohawk Language Learning Resources (CC BY 4.0) · Six Nations Polytechnic

english: i am studying Mohawk / I am learning Kanien'kéha
notes: I am studying Kanien'kéha — Kateweyénhstha (I am studying/learning) + Kanyen'kéha (the language, Six Nations orthographic form). Note Kanyen'kéha vs Kanien'kéha — the y/i variation is a dialect difference between Six Nations and Kanehsatà:ke. Both are correct.

cite.text: eCampusOntario OER — Mohawk Language Learning Resources (CC BY 4.0) · Six Nations Polytechnic

notes: Hello, is it going well? — An alternate greeting combining the hello + wellness question in one phrase.

cite.text: eCampusOntario OER — Mohawk Language Learning Resources (CC BY 4.0) · Six Nations Polytechnic

notes: ___ is my age — ___ na'tewakohseriyá:konh. The full formal age introduction formula from the eCampusOntario OER. Compare Tsi niiá:wen ní:'i? (how old are you?) which you know.

cite.text: eCampusOntario OER — Mohawk Language Learning Resources (CC BY 4.0) · Six Nations Polytechnic

english: I am of the Turtle Clan
notes: I am of the Turtle Clan — wakenyáhten. The wa- prefix marks first person; -ken- is the Turtle Clan root; -yáhten marks clan membership.

cite.text: Mohawk Language Learning Resources (CC BY 4.0) · Six Nations Polytechnic · Stevie Jonathan, Tehakanere Henhawk, Jersey Squire

english: you are of the Turtle Clan
notes: You are of the Turtle Clan — sanyáhten. The sa- prefix marks second person singular.

cite.text: Mohawk Language Learning Resources (CC BY 4.0) · Six Nations Polytechnic · Stevie Jonathan, Tehakanere Henhawk, Jersey Squire

english: I am of the Wolf Clan
notes: I am of the Wolf Clan — wakathahyón:ni. The -thahyón:ni root marks Wolf Clan membership.

cite.text: Mohawk Language Learning Resources (CC BY 4.0) · Six Nations Polytechnic · Stevie Jonathan, Tehakanere Henhawk, Jersey Squire

english: I am of the Bear Clan
notes: I am of the Bear Clan — wakhskaré:wake. The -hskaré:wake root marks Bear Clan membership.

cite.text: Mohawk Language Learning Resources (CC BY 4.0) · Six Nations Polytechnic · Stevie Jonathan, Tehakanere Henhawk, Jersey Squire

notes: What clan are you? — Oh nisen'tarò:ten? A key conversational phrase for introductions in Kanien'kehá:ka communities where clan membership is central to identity.

cite.text: Mohawk Language Learning Resources (CC BY 4.0) · Six Nations Polytechnic · Stevie Jonathan, Tehakanere Henhawk, Jersey Squire

english: That is where the sightseeing place is
She/he will be back at [number] o'clock.
She/he is gone for the week.
Would you please sit down? Yes, thank you.
Would you please give me some water? Yes, I'll go and get it.
Niionatá:'a bought the house I had
My mother won one million dollars
My mother is calling me in to eat
The plate has a floral design
The pet I used to have looks like yours
The zebra live in the south
My grandmother wants to take shade from the sun
My grandmother is coming to visit tomorrow
We live near the Ottawa River.
I play with my older sister
I gave my dress that I had to my younger sister
We have all finished our work.

target: Akwé:kon kenh skátne sewaiá:tare tsi sewatswà:tha?
english: Are you all on the same team?
accepts: Akwé:kon kenh skátne sewaiá:tare tsi sewatswà:tha?
akwé:kon kenh skátne sewaiá:tare tsi sewatswà:tha?

english: Your purse is on the chair
The chair is made of wood
The groundhog is peeking from his hole
I bought a hat and mittens
The muskrat eats frogs and roots
The gray squirrel has a mean temper
Can you call and ask his/her mother?
She/he is gone for the week.
The little girl is wearing a pink dress

target: Aón:ton kenh iaonsáhshien ne ká:sere tsi nón:we tió:wen?
english: Can you put the cart back where it belongs?
accepts: Aón:ton kenh iaonsáhshien ne ká:sere tsi nón:we tió:wen?
aón:ton kenh iaonsáhshien ne ká:sere tsi nón:we tió:wen?

english: The squirrel is eating a nut
I like the taste of salad.

target: Asé'stsi' kénh kí:ken áhta'? Hén, thetén:re wa'kní:non'.
english: Are these shoes new? Yes, I bought them yesterday.
accepts: Asé'stsi' kénh kí:ken áhta'? Hén, thetén:re wa'kní:non'.
asé'stsi' kénh kí:ken áhta'? hén, thetén:re wa'kní:non'.

english: There are tables at the restaurant
Put the corn bread on the table. What else?
The belt is made of leather.
Go down and look for it
Your grandson is coming this way
The little girl is wearing a pink dress
Put it away when you're done.
Can you help me pick the leaves up?
Can you help me make apple pie?

target: Enhshnekí:ra' kénh skawí:sa onòn:ta'? Iáh, nía:wen.
english: Would you like a glass of milk? No, thank you.
accepts: Enhshnekí:ra' kénh skawí:sa onòn:ta'? Iáh, nía:wen.
enhshnekí:ra' kénh skawí:sa onòn:ta'? iáh, nía:wen.

target: Enskwé:ni' kenh enhsekhr'ó:ri nahó:ten sewatswá:tha aonhá:'a son'wéskwani?
english: Can you tell me about a sport that you like?
accepts: Enskwé:ni' kenh enhsekhr'ó:ri nahó:ten sewatswá:tha aonhá:'a son'wéskwani?
enskwé:ni' kenh enhsekhr'ó:ri nahó:ten sewatswá:tha aonhá:'a son'wéskwani?

target: Enskwé:ni' kenh enhseweientéhta'ne tsi niío:re enióhrhen'ne? Iáh, takkwén:ni.
english: Can you learn this by tomorrow? No, I can't.
accepts: Enskwé:ni' kenh enhseweientéhta'ne tsi niío:re enióhrhen'ne? Iáh, takkwén:ni.
enskwé:ni' kenh enhseweientéhta'ne tsi niío:re enióhrhen'ne? iáh, takkwén:ni.

target: Enwá:ton ken teniakenithá:re ne… Hen, kwah wátskhok. Iáh, ken té:iens / té:res.
english: Can I speak to… Yes, one minute. No, she/he is not here.
accepts: Enwá:ton ken teniakenithá:re ne… Hen, kwah wátskhok. Iáh, ken té:iens / té:res.
enwá:ton ken teniakenithá:re ne… hen, kwah wátskhok. iáh, ken té:iens / té:res.

english: It's time to take a bath/shower.
Get the knife and peel them.
Please get some milk and bread.
There are a lot of people here today.

target: Iáh kenh ohná:ken tha'esaiá'tenhá:wihte wahsateriwaienhstá:ne? Iáh, tenkaráhtate.
english: Won't you be late for school? No, I'll run.
accepts: Iáh kenh ohná:ken tha'esaiá'tenhá:wihte wahsateriwaienhstá:ne? Iáh, tenkaráhtate.
iáh kenh ohná:ken tha'esaiá'tenhá:wihte wahsateriwaienhstá:ne? iáh, tenkaráhtate.

english: She/he will not be back today.
It's time to wait for the bus.
There's enough snow to go sliding.
I use an umbrella when it rains.
I am learning to play the clarinet.
Where are you going? I'm going to the store.
Where did you put the book? I put it on the table.

target: Ka'niká:ien ne oká:ra tkaráhstha aonhá:'a senón:we's?
accepts: Ka'niká:ien ne oká:ra tkaráhstha aonhá:'a senón:we's?
ka'niká:ien ne oká:ra tkaráhstha aonhá:'a senón:we's?

english: Where are the children? They are playing.
Where is the ruler? It's on the table.
Where is your sweater? I lost it.
Where do you live? Near the school.
I am eating bread and soup.
The houses are nice where we live.
He wants to buy a house.
Would you like to watch a video?
It's sleeting big pieces of sleet.
You will write your phone number here
An owl lives in my barn.
What is this? This is a winter coat.
What's your name? My name is Mary.
What is this? This is a record player.
What are you using? I'm using crayons.
What do you like the best?
What do you want to be for Halloween?
What do you get at Halloween?
